name: Saudi Connection 911
description: The 'Saudi Connection 911' is a conspiracy theory alleging that the government of Saudi Arabia, or elements within it, provided financial or logistical support to the al-Qaeda hijackers responsible for the September 11, 2001 attacks. Proponents argue that evidence such as alleged financial links between Saudi officials and some of the hijackers, as well as classified portions of the 9/11 Commission Report (the '28 pages'), suggest a deeper involvement than officially acknowledged. The theory gained traction in the years following the attacks, fueled by survivor groups, investigative journalists, and political figures. Mainstream investigations, including the 9/11 Commission, found no direct evidence that the Saudi government as a whole knowingly supported the attacks. However, the Commission noted that some individual Saudi officials may have had contact with individuals linked to al-Qaeda. Critics point to the lack of conclusive evidence implicating the Saudi government and argue that the theory is often based on circumstantial evidence and politically motivated accusations. The release of the redacted '28 pages' in 2016 did not provide definitive proof of official Saudi involvement, though it did detail contacts between Saudi officials and some of the hijackers, prompting further debate and investigation. The debate continues to be politically sensitive, given the complex relationship between the United States and Saudi Arabia.
subtitle: Alleged Saudi support for 9/11 hijackers

claims.claim: Saudi officials knowingly funded al-Qaeda hijackers.
claims.evidence: Financial transactions traced from Saudi Arabia to individuals who aided some of the hijackers.
claims.criticism: These transactions are often circumstantial and do not definitively prove knowledge or intent to support terrorism. The 9/11 Commission found no direct evidence of Saudi government funding of al-Qaeda.

claims.claim: The '28 pages' of the 9/11 Commission Report contain irrefutable proof of Saudi government involvement.
claims.evidence: The redacted pages detail contacts between Saudi officials and individuals linked to the hijackers.
claims.criticism: The 9/11 Commission investigated these contacts and concluded that they did not constitute evidence of official Saudi government support for the attacks. The released pages are open to interpretation.

claims.claim: The US government is covering up the full extent of Saudi Arabia's involvement to protect the strategic alliance between the two countries.
claims.evidence: Continued secrecy surrounding certain documents and the close relationship between the US and Saudi Arabia.
claims.criticism: The US government has released numerous documents related to 9/11 and has conducted multiple investigations. Maintaining a relationship with Saudi Arabia is seen as vital for US foreign policy and energy security, regardless of conspiracy claims.

keyFigures.name: Bob Graham
keyFigures.role: Former US Senator who advocated for the release of the '28 pages' and investigation of Saudi ties.

keyFigures.name: Philip Zelikow
keyFigures.role: Executive Director of the 9/11 Commission, which investigated the allegations and found no direct evidence of Saudi government involvement.

timeline.event: The Joint Congressional Inquiry into 9/11 releases its report, including the '28 pages' which remain classified.
The 9/11 Commission releases its final report, concluding no direct Saudi government involvement.
The '28 pages' are declassified and released to the public.

sources.author: Kirkpatrick, David D.
sources.work: 28 Pages, Long Secret, Hint at Saudi Ties to 9/11 Hijackers
sources.title: Kirkpatrick, David D.

sources.author: Thomas Kean and Lee Hamilton
sources.work: Without Precedent: The Inside Story of the 9/11 Commission
sources.title: Thomas Kean and Lee Hamilton
